There is currently no mechanisme in the product to remove all occurrences of 'ALL' from sudoers. A mechanism to report, but not change, the existence of this statement in sudoers will be implemented in a future release.

The product parses the output of 'sshd -T' for the key that the system uses for communication and ensures that key is appropriately protected. The other keys in that location may not have the correct permissions applied. https://github.com/simp/pupmod-simp-ssh/issues/150 has been created to address this issue.

This check will fail if there is a commented out line that looks like "#PrintLastLog = <value>" even if the correct value of "PrintLastLog = true" exists in the /etc/ssh/sshd_config file.

The product cannot determine where audit aliases will be stored, the default location will be /etc/aliases, however, if we configure this and users configure the aliases elsewhere there will be no meaningful changes made.

The fixtext for this rule conflicts with the actual check being done, the regex the rule is looking for also appears to be incorrect. The rule is remediated according to what's being requested in the fixtext.

The product will set the mode of the files referenced in this rule to "700" and "600" rather then "750" and "640". This will lock down the files and keep them secure so only admins and ISSMs can access them.

There is currently no way for the product to extrapolate which audit tools are installed on the system. Since there is no way to know which tools are in use, permissions for those tools won't be controlled by the product.

The scan rule will fail because the module being used currently places a space between the equals sign and the key/value being set in the logind.conf file. There is currently a pull request in the github module being used that should address this issue and prevent future failures - https://github.com/simp/pupmod-voxpupuli-systemd/pull/2.
